title: Ozel Splits CHP to Launch New Party, S-400 Transfer Talks Surface, Turkey-Egypt Military Drills Deepen
subhead: Turkey's opposition fractured on Tuesday as ousted CHP leader Ozgur Ozel announced he is forming a breakaway party, a move that could reshape the opposition landscape ahead of 2028 elections. Defense Minister Guler confirmed S-400 transfer talks with third countries, and Turkey-Egypt joint military exercises signaled a strategic realignment in the Eastern Mediterranean.

sections.heading: Ozel Announces Breakaway Party After Court-Ouster From CHP
sections.body:
Ozgur Ozel, the court-removed leader of Turkey's main opposition Republican People's Party (CHP), announced on Tuesday that he is resigning from the party and forming a new one — a move that formally splits Turkey's largest opposition bloc. Speaking to more than 80 CHP lawmakers in parliament, Ozel said: 'We are establishing our new party by taking the first step together with the deputies chosen by our nation. These are the determined steps of a march toward governing Turkey.'

The new party, widely expected to be called Yeni Parti (New Party), will be formally launched later this week, with Ozel reportedly set to submit an application to the interior ministry on Monday. Political insiders told AFP that between 83 and 85 of the CHP's 135 lawmakers are expected to defect to the new faction. If that happens, the CHP would be left with around 50 seats — falling behind the pro-Kurdish DEM Party's 56 seats.

Ozel was removed as CHP chair in May after an Ankara court annulled the party's November 2023 leadership congress, citing alleged irregularities, and reinstated the unpopular former leader Kemal Kilicdaroglu. Critics — including Ozel himself — call the ruling a politically motivated 'coup' designed to weaken the opposition. Ozel said he would not 'leave behind' jailed Istanbul Mayor Ekrem Imamoglu or Ankara Mayor Mansur Yavas, both seen as potential presidential challengers to Erdogan.

Pollster Murat Gezici warned that while the new party could eventually become the main opposition hub, 'in the short and medium-term, the political arithmetic works in favour of Erdogan and the AK Party.' Under Turkey's constitution, Erdogan can only run for president again if parliament calls an early election before May 2028, which requires a three-fifths majority — 360 of 600 seats. The AKP and its allies currently hold 328.
sections.whyItMatters: The CHP split is the biggest shake-up in Turkish opposition politics in years. In the near term, it fractures the anti-Erdogan vote and strengthens the president's hand. But if Ozel can consolidate the reformist wing and attract DEM Party voters disillusioned with the old CHP, it could produce a more dynamic opposition — though that remains a big if given the legal and political headwinds.

sections.heading: Defense Minister Confirms S-400 Transfer Talks With Third Countries
sections.body:
Defense Minister Yasar Guler confirmed in written responses to Grand National Assembly members that Turkey is in talks with third countries over the future of its Russian-made S-400 air defense systems, the clearest public acknowledgment yet that a transfer deal is under discussion. Guler said discussions with Washington were continuing 'in a positive atmosphere,' adding that solutions were being sought 'with mutual understanding and cooperation, for issues that are pending from the past and also concern third countries.'

The confirmation follows weeks of media reports that Ankara has finalized a deal to resell its S-400 systems to the UAE. Pro-government Hurriyet columnist Abdulkadir Selvi reported that 'technical and procedural issues' had been settled and an official announcement was expected but postponed at the last minute. Kremlin spokesman Dmitry Peskov confirmed Moscow is holding active talks on reselling the systems, calling the matter 'extremely sensitive.' Russian sources described Moscow's internal position as 'why not' — pragmatic consent rather than enthusiasm.

However, former Defense Minister and current parliamentary defense committee chair Hulusi Akar issued a sharp denial on July 18, stating: 'We did not say and are not saying that the S-400s are being sent to the UAE.' Akar added that the dispute with Washington is 'close to resolution.' The contradiction between pro-government leaks and a direct legislative denial underscores the diplomatic sensitivity of the issue.

President Erdogan addressed the matter only by saying: 'Continue to watch us.' During the July 7-8 NATO summit in Ankara, President Trump signaled readiness to lift CAATSA sanctions on Turkey, telling reporters Washington does not want to 'impose sanctions on friends.'

If completed, the transfer would resolve the single biggest friction point in US-Turkey relations. Turkey was removed from the F-35 program in 2019 over the S-400 purchase, losing $9 billion in defense industry contracts and access to six fully built F-35A fighters worth approximately $480 million now sitting in US storage. Under Section 1245 of the NDAA, Turkey can only re-enter the F-35 program if it completely renounces ownership or operation of S-400 systems.
sections.whyItMatters: The S-400-to-UAE transfer, if confirmed, would be the first time a NATO member re-exports sanctioned Russian strategic weapons to a Gulf partner. It would unlock Turkey's return to the F-35 program and remove CAATSA sanctions — two monumental shifts in Turkey's defense posture. But the gap between leaks and Akar's denial suggests negotiations are still in play, and both Moscow's approval and US legal requirements remain unresolved.

sections.heading: Turkey-Egypt Joint Military Exercises Signal Strategic Realignment
sections.body:
Combat training footage from Egyptian military facilities this month revealed that Turkey and Egypt have launched large-scale joint military exercises under the codename 'Golden Eagle,' involving elite paratrooper units, special forces, and Thunder commandos from both countries. The drills follow a major defense framework agreement signed during Erdogan's visit to Cairo and represent the second major escalation in bilateral military cooperation this summer.

In late June, the two countries completed joint air force exercises at multiple Egyptian airbases, involving F-16s from both air forces. Egypt then deployed forces to Turkey's Anatolian Eagle 2026 trilateral air exercises with Azerbaijan and NATO forces, practicing coordinated strikes, electronic warfare, and command-and-control procedures.

The rapid transition from air drills to ground commando operations signals what Israeli outlet Ynet described as a 'cold, cynical strategic alliance between two authoritarian heavyweights' — a characterization that reflects Israel's alarm at the deepening military relationship. According to the report, Egypt is exploring participation in Turkey's KAAN stealth fighter program and signing agreements with Turkish defense firms to localize production of advanced drones.
sections.whyItMatters: The Turkey-Egypt military rapprochement is happening at remarkable speed — from estranged rivals to joint special forces exercises within two years. For Israel, it raises the prospect of Turkish military positioning near its southern border. For the Eastern Mediterranean, it upends the balance that has kept Turkey and Egypt on opposite sides of gas, maritime, and Libya dossiers. The KAAN and drone cooperation, if real, would mark a significant defense-industrial partnership.

sections.heading: Fitch Affirms Turkey at BB-, Flags Reserves as Key to Upgrade
sections.body:
Fitch Ratings affirmed Turkey's sovereign credit rating at BB- with a stable outlook on Friday, with Senior Director Erich Arispe Morales saying the economy 'maintains its resilience despite global uncertainties and high geopolitical risks.' Fitch highlighted Turkey's tight monetary policy as having preserved inflation gains, with dollarization stabilizing at around 38% and real interest rates supporting lira-denominated asset attractiveness.

However, Fitch flagged international reserves as the critical factor for any potential upgrade. Morales noted that reserves fell during the initial phase of the US-Iran war and have since partially recovered, but remain below pre-war levels. 'Whether Turkey can achieve a significant and lasting improvement in international reserves will be decisive' for a potential positive rating action, he said.

Fitch projects the Central Bank will cut its policy rate by a cumulative 200 basis points by year-end, bringing it to 35%, based on assumptions that geopolitical risks will ease and energy prices will decline. Inflation remains above 30%, though signs of deceleration continue. The banking sector's health and access to external financing were cited as factors providing resilience against external shocks.

Meanwhile, Borsa Istanbul saw its investor count surpass 6.8 million, up 462,000 in three weeks, driven by renewed IPO activity, according to Turkish media.
sections.whyItMatters: Fitch's stable affirmation with a reserves-linked upgrade path gives Ankara a modest vote of confidence at a volatile moment. But the conditionality is clear: sustained reserve accumulation and continued inflation reduction are prerequisites. The projected 200 bps of easing by year-end assumes the Iran ceasefire holds and energy prices fall — assumptions that remain uncertain.

sections.heading: G7 Leaders Discuss Iran Deal, Trump Downplays Ukraine Impact on US
sections.body:
At the G7 summit in France, European leaders raised concerns about the US-Iran interim deal's implications while pressing Trump to reconsider his approach to Ukraine. Trump downplayed the Ukraine war's significance for the US, saying it 'has no impact' on America, even as allied leaders pushed for stronger collective action.

Trump vowed to release the full text of the Iran agreement within days, though an AP analysis found that White House talking points about the deal did not match its actual terms. European leaders used the summit to test the deal's risks and advocate for tighter enforcement mechanisms.

Oil prices fell 5% to a three-month low on Strait of Hormuz reopening hopes, though mine-clearing operations could delay full shipping resumption by 40-50 days. Brent crude stood at approximately $88 per barrel, down from wartime highs but still elevated.
sections.whyItMatters: For Turkey, the G7 dynamics matter on two fronts: the Iran ceasefire framework — if it holds — would ease energy import costs that Fitch flagged as a key vulnerability, but Trump's dismissal of Ukraine complicates Turkey's own mediation ambitions with Moscow. The oil price decline helps Turkey's current account, though the benefit is tempered by the continued risk premium on shipping through Hormuz.

sections.heading: Iran's Araghchi Credits Turkey With Blocking Kurdish Offensive
sections.body:
Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi confirmed in a video interview with Iranian outlet Majaraa Media that Turkey played a 'very effective' role in preventing Iranian Kurdish armed groups based in Iraq's Kurdistan Region from joining the US-Israeli war against Iran. Araghchi said that after learning that Trump had contacted leaders of Iranian Kurdish groups on the fourth day of the war, he reached out to Iraqi Kurdish leaders, the Iraqi prime minister, and Turkish Foreign Minister Hakan Fidan.

'Turkey held talks with the Americans and took a very firm position on the issue,' Araghchi said. 'These collective efforts ensured the failure of the plot to divide Iran and stir internal unrest.' He also accused Reza Pahlavi, son of Iran's last shah, of reaching an agreement with Israel to partition Iran.

The confirmation corroborates earlier reports: a New York Times analysis in March said an Israeli plan to use Iranian Kurdish militias to trigger unrest in northwestern Iran had been blocked by Turkey. Israeli opposition leader Yair Lapid criticized Netanyahu for pursuing the plan without considering Erdogan's response, saying Erdogan had 'taught Netanyahu a big lesson.'
sections.whyItMatters: This is now Tehran's first on-the-record confirmation of Turkey's direct role in blocking a second front in the Iran war. It cements Turkey's credibility as a regional power broker — but also creates a paper trail that could complicate future relations with both Washington and Kurdish groups. The disclosure reframes Turkey's wartime stance from passive neutrality to active shaping of the conflict's trajectory.

bottomLine: The biggest domestic development today is Ozel's break from the CHP — it formally fractures Turkey's main opposition at a moment when Erdogan's legal crackdown on opposition figures has already jailed Istanbul's mayor and 27 other CHP-elected officials. The split could produce a more dynamic opposition long-term, but in the near term it almost certainly benefits the AKP. On defense, Guler's confirmation that S-400 transfer talks are underway — coupled with conflicting signals from Akar — suggests a deal is in advanced stages but not yet sealed. The Turkey-Egypt military exercises mark an unexpectedly rapid strategic realignment in the Eastern Mediterranean that has Israel watching closely. And Araghchi's public confirmation of Turkey's role in blocking the Kurdish front solidifies Ankara's post-war diplomatic capital, even as it creates potential complications with Washington and Kurdish interlocutors.
sources: Reuters, AP, AFP, Al-Monitor, Daily Sabah, Courthouse News, Bianet, Cyprus Mail, Defence Security Asia, News.am, Ynet, Dunya, Anadolu Agency, Fitch Ratings, Bloomberg
